title: Chocolate sandwich cake
subtitle: A classic two-layer British chocolate sponge with buttercream
excerpt: A chocolate sandwich cake is two equal sponge layers filled and topped with chocolate buttercream. The method is the all-in-one creaming method: all the ingredients go into one bowl. No splitting, no layering, no decoration beyond a smooth topping of buttercream.
sourceNotes: The British chocolate sandwich cake follows the same structure as the Victoria sponge, with cocoa powder replacing some of the flour. The all-in-one method, in which softened butter and all other ingredients are beaten together simultaneously, was popularised in Britain in the 1970s when soft margarine became widely available. It produces an evenly textured sponge suitable for a beginner. This recipe follows the established British tradition.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze the unfilled sponge layers individually, wrapped well, for up to 2 months. Thaw at room temperature before filling.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Bake the sponges a day ahead and fill on the day of serving. The buttercream can be made 2 days ahead and stored in a covered bowl at room temperature.

glossaryTerms.definition: The all-in-one method puts all ingredients into the bowl at the same time and beats them together. It is faster than the creaming method and produces a tender, even sponge because the fat coats the flour before the liquid is added, limiting gluten development. The butter must be soft enough to blend easily; cold butter will not combine properly and the batter will look curdled.

content.text: makes this an ideal first cake. Everything goes into the bowl together, you beat until smooth, and it goes straight into the tins. The chocolate buttercream filling is made while the sponges cool. The whole thing, start to finish, takes about 90 minutes including cooling time.

Heat the oven to 180 degrees C. Grease two 20 cm round tins and line the bases. Sift the 175 g self-raising flour, 30 g cocoa and 1 tsp baking powder into a large bowl. Add the 175 g sugar, 175 g softened butter, 3 eggs and 3 tbsp milk. Beat with a hand mixer on medium speed for 2 minutes until smooth, pale and slightly fluffy. Divide between the two tins. Bake for 22 to 26 minutes until the sponges spring back when pressed in the centre and are pulling away from the tin sides. Cool in the tins for 5 minutes, then turn out onto a wire rack and leave until completely cold.

Beat the 150 g softened butter until very pale, about 3 minutes. Sift in the 250 g icing sugar and 30 g cocoa and beat until smooth, adding the 2 tbsp milk to loosen. Place one cooled sponge on a plate. Spread half the buttercream over the top. Place the second sponge on top and spread the remaining buttercream over it.

items.solution: The oven door was opened too early, the oven was too cool, or the batter was overmixed after the flour went in. Use a timer and do not open the door before 20 minutes. The sponge should be springy and pulling from the sides when done.

The icing sugar was not sifted or the butter was cold. Sift both the icing sugar and cocoa thoroughly. The butter must be soft enough to press flat with a finger; straight from the fridge it will not blend properly.
